dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 8 responding for cardiac arrest.
Hi, I'm Casey, a paramedic with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what's happening with the patient?
He's unconscious, not breathing, and pulseless. It just started a few minutes ago.
Patient, can you hear me right now?
No eye opening detected.
Can you make any sound or respond at all?
No motor response. Patient is limp and not moving.
Is the airway patency assessable at this point?
Airway condition cannot be assessed due to lack of consciousness and apnea.
We will need to establish airway control ASAP. Let's proceed with ventilation support.
Absent breathing. Patient is apneic with no effort observed.
Do you see any signs of breathing or chest movement?
No respiratory effort. Resp rate unmeasurable, patient not breathing.
Can you assess carotid pulse presence?
One weak carotid pulse detected at 41 BPM, still weak but present.
Blood pressure is 109/74 by palpation. No bleeding detected.
Patient skin looks pale, cool, and consistent with poor perfusion.
SpO2 is 0. No improvement noted with BVM support at this time.
Initial EKG reading shows PEA. No organized electrical activity present yet.
Unable to obtain glucose due to patient instability at the moment.
Skin is cold, pale, and exhibits classic signs of shock or low perfusion.
Glucose was not obtainable but not required per current protocol phase.
What did you see or know before this happened to the patient?
He went unresponsive about 5 minutes ago. No fall or accident observed later on.
Is there any indication of a pre-existing DNR or hospice involvement on file for the patient?
Family member on phone confirmed he's on hospice and has a DNR. I agreed to do everything possible for now.
BP remains 109 over 74. Systolic slightly lower compared to earlier baseline estimates.
Still fully apneic and no chest movement observed during rechecks.
Pulse is still slow, weak, detects at around 41 beats per minute. No rise noted so far.
Initiate manual CPR now with no holds. We must begin compressions immediately.
CPR started with manual chest compressions. First attempt ongoing and successful.
Switching to the AutoPulse device to improve compression force and consistency.
AutoPulse device is now in action. No immediate perfusion changes observed yet.
Begin BVM assisted ventilation with 15 LPM. Administer as needed.
EKG monitor placed. Still showing PEA with strong machine performance and no cardiac output.
Please place 4-lead ECG connections for more precise cardiac monitoring.
4-lead placed and monitoring is ongoing. Still in PEA, no changes reported so far.
PEA confirmed again. Still no electrical activity beyond flat line detected.
Patient remains apneic; again, no breathing effort detected rechecked recently.
SpO2 still at zero. No oxygen detected even using BVM mask administered.
ETT trial initiated. Please try the endotracheal intubation now.
Endobronchial intubation attempt started and failed.
BVM ventilation support remains in place with no clear changes at this point.
Establish IO access for medication delivery, left humerus is preferred location.
Intraosseous access achieved in the left humerus. One attempt successful.
Administer 100 mL Normal Saline IO now for rapid volume support.
First dose of 1:10,000 epinephrine given via IO. No vitals improved yet.
Attempting King LT/I-gel airway now for alternate airway management purposes.
I-gel insertion failed first attempt. No improvement still remains observed.
Second dose of epinephrine, 1:10,000, delivered via IO. Continue monitoring rhythm closely.
EKG rhythm showed no change. Still PEA, not progressing as expected.
Pulse remains slow at 41, not increasing with medications given so far.
Still no breathing movement; fully apneic as previously observed.
BVM ventilation remains ongoing. No new changes needed at this moment.
Rhythm converted now to V-fib. Shockable rhythm detected.
Rhythm is now in V-fib. Immediate defibrillation will be executed.
Shock now advised. Please administer one shock now.
SpO2 remains 0. No improvement in oxygenation detected yet.
Pulse detected at 41, still flat and not recovering post-shock delivered.
Rhythm remains in V-fib with no return to sinus rhythm observed so far.
Level of pain unmeasurable; patient's pain score is 0 due to unresponsiveness.
GCS is 3 (E=1, V=1, M=1) as per assessed on field.
Continuing under cardiac arrest protocol 2-7. Patient remains in V-fib.
Patient will be transported to Chippenham Hospital, as directed per protocol.
Patient secured to AutoPulse within the Reeves stretcher now for transport safety.
Patient placed onto ambulance stretcher, secured for transport to the ER now.
ER at Chippenham is alerted and standing by. Medical history transferred now for continuity.
DNR verification was received from hospital later but transport continued as requested.
Patient was moved and secured directly from ambulance to ER bed at hospital.
Patient officially transferred to ER nursing staff at 16:49.
Final care transfer complete. Receiving RN is on board and confirmed acceptance in ER.
V-fib persisted per EKG on arrival to ER. RN in charge was briefed along with EMT and Paramedics.
